one_line_takeaway: SlotGCG replaces GCG's fixed suffix-insertion assumption with an attention-derived Vulnerable Slot Score that identifies and targets the most jailbreak-susceptible token positions anywhere in a prompt, yielding average ASR gains of roughly +6 to +25 percentage points over five GCG-family baselines, faster convergence (e.g. 138->40 iterations on Llama-2-7B/GCG), and materially higher (but not universal -- Perplexity Filter still fully blocks it) robustness to input-filtering defenses.
key_finding: Averaged across six target models, SlotGCG improves Attack Success Rate over each corresponding GCG-family baseline by: GCG +13.3pp (66.7%->80.0%), AttnGCG +24.6pp (61.7%->86.3%), I-GCG +12.7pp (73.0%->85.7%), GCG-Hij +6.3pp (78.0%->84.3%), and GBDA +19.7pp (20.3%->40.0%) (Table 1). The largest single-model gain reported is on Llama-2-13B with AttnGCG, +62pp (20.0%->82.0%). Under six defenses (Table 3), SlotGCG raises average ASR over baseline GCG-family attacks by roughly +23 to +33 percentage points depending on the base attack (e.g., GCG average ASR under defenses rises from 18.9% to 46.2%, a +27.3pp gain; I-GCG rises from 21.8% to 53.6%, +31.8pp), attributed to SlotGCG's more dispersed VSS distribution across slots (Table 2: mean attention 3.721e-3 for GCG vs 3.933e-3 for SlotGCG, with lower standard deviation for SlotGCG: 3.874e-3 vs 4.807e-3), making it more resilient when some inserted tokens are removed or perturbed by a defense. SlotGCG also substantially reduces the number of optimization steps needed to reach a successful jailbreak (Table 4): e.g. on Llama-2-7B, average iterations-to-success for GCG drop from 138.11 to 40.50, and for AttnGCG from 131.61 to 25.98 (up to roughly 10x speedup in some cells), though two model/attack cells (Vicuna-7B+GCG and Qwen-2.5+GCG) show a small increase in iteration count.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is SlotGCG, an extension of the GCG optimization-based jailbreak attack that abandons the fixed suffix-insertion assumption. The authors formalize 'slots' (L+1 candidate insertion positions within a prompt, following the Insertion Transformer definition of Stern et al. 2019) and introduce the Vulnerable Slot Score (VSS), a metric computed from attention weights (summed over upper-half transformer layers and heads, from after-chat-template tokens to inserted adversarial tokens, normalized by inserted-token count) that quantifies how susceptible a given slot is to adversarial insertion. SlotGCG is a 4-step pipeline: (1) probe every slot by inserting a probing token into each of the L+1 positions simultaneously, (2) compute VSS for each probed slot and convert to an insertion-probability distribution via softmax with temperature T, (3) allocate an adversarial token budget m across slots proportionally to that distribution (using largest-remainder rounding), (4) run GCG-based gradient-guided coordinate optimization on the resulting multi-slot adversarial tokens. The authors state SlotGCG adds only ~200ms of preprocessing (a single extra inference pass for probing) and is attack-agnostic, i.e. pluggable into any optimization-based jailbreak method's optimization step.
threat_model: White-box optimization-based jailbreak attacker with full access to model weights/gradients (the GCG threat model), targeting open-weight instruction-tuned chat LLMs (Llama-2-7B/13B, Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, Mistral-7B, Vicuna-7B, Qwen-2.5). The attacker's goal is to append/insert adversarial token sequences into a harmful prompt so the model produces a compliant harmful completion (e.g., 'Sure, here is a tutorial for making a bomb'), evaluated on the 50-behavior AdvBench harmful-behaviors set. The paper additionally evaluates the attack against six defense mechanisms (Perplexity Filter, Erase-and-Check suffix/infusion variants, SmoothLLM swap/insert/patch variants, RPO, SafeDecoding, Llama-Guard-3), i.e. a threat model where the defender has an input-filtering or output-moderation layer in front of the model.
limitations: Perplexity Filter defense drives ASR to 0.0% for both baseline and SlotGCG-augmented attacks across all four tested attack families in Table 3, i.e. SlotGCG provides no measured robustness benefit against that specific defense. RPO shows mixed/marginal results, with two of four attack combinations (GCG and GCG-Hij) actually decreasing slightly under SlotGCG (-2.0pp and -4.0pp respectively). Against Llama-Guard-3 the gains are small (0 to +8pp). The paper itself flags a counter-intuitive artifact: under some defenses, ASR for both baseline and SlotGCG can be higher than in the no-defense condition, which the authors attribute to the GPT-4-based early-stopping evaluation protocol being bypassed when weak/marginal attacks are blocked by the defense before reaching the GPT-4 judge, letting optimization continue toward a more clearly harmful (and more reliably judged) output -- a limitation of their own ASR measurement methodology, not just of the defenses. All core experiments use only the 50-behavior AdvBench subset; the larger 388-behavior transfer evaluation (Universal SlotGCG) is only briefly summarized qualitatively in the main text with detailed numbers pushed to an appendix not covered by the paper. All white-box experiments assume attacker access to the specific victim model's weights/gradients (the GCG threat model), so results do not establish black-box or transfer effectiveness against closed-weight/production LLMs. VSS computation and the probing step still require attention-weight access to the target model, so the method's applicability is limited to models where internals are inspectable.

datasets_used: AdvBench harmful-behaviors dataset (Zou et al. 2023), 50-behavior subset used for the exploratory studies and main experiments
AdvBench 388-behavior transfer set (Zou et al. 2023) used for the Universal SlotGCG zero-shot transfer evaluation
quantitative_results: Average ASR gain of SlotGCG over base GCG: 66.7% -> 80.0% (+13.3pp)
Average ASR gain of SlotGCG over base AttnGCG: 61.7% -> 86.3% (+24.6pp)
Average ASR gain of SlotGCG over base I-GCG: 73.0% -> 85.7% (+12.7pp)
Average ASR gain of SlotGCG over base GCG-Hij: 78.0% -> 84.3% (+6.3pp)
Average ASR gain of SlotGCG over base GBDA: 20.3% -> 40.0% (+19.7pp)
Largest single gain: Llama-2-13B + AttnGCG, 20.0% -> 82.0% ASR (+62pp)
Perplexity Filter defense: 0.0% ASR for both base and +Ours across all four tested attacks (no improvement)
Mean adversarial-token attention (Table 2): GCG 3.721e-3 (std 4.807e-3) vs SlotGCG 3.933e-3 (std 3.874e-3)
Iterations-to-success on Llama-2-7B: GCG 138.11->40.50, AttnGCG 131.61->25.98, I-GCG 123.16->19.14, GCG-Hij 78.47->35.02
Average iterations-to-success across all models: GCG 72.59->28.59, AttnGCG 75.73->20.21, I-GCG 66.52->20.11, GCG-Hij 59.65->25.25
VSSinit vs VSSfinal correlation across 50 prompts ranged 0.4 to 0.9, supporting Finding 3 (vulnerable slots are inherent to the prompt, not an artifact of optimization)
SlotGCG preprocessing overhead: approximately 200ms (single extra probing inference pass) per the abstract/introduction
baselines_compared: Defenses: Perplexity Filter (Alon & Kamfonas 2023), Erase-and-Check suffix/infusion (Kumar et al. 2023), SmoothLLM swap/insert/patch (Robey et al. 2023), RPO (Zhou et al. 2024), SafeDecoding (Xu et al. 2024), Llama-Guard-3 (Grattafiori et al. 2024)
